text: The surgeon develops the right retro-rectus space, following the completed incision of the right posterior rectus sheath, in preparation for the bilateral TAR procedure. The ports are inserted in the left lateral retro-rectus space, medial to the left linea semilunaris.

The surgeon approaches the area below the arcuate line, demonstrating the attenuation of the posterior rectus sheath. The dissection reveals multiple layers beneath the arcuate line, including the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum. The surgeon begins a bottoms-up TAR, entering the preperitoneal space where preperitoneal fat separates easily from the lateral abdominal wall. The inguinal portion of the dissection is briefly addressed, noting a second arcuate line of attenuated posterior rectus tissue.

The surgeon enters the desired preperitoneal space, revealing preperitoneal fat that separates easily from the lateral abdominal wall. The transverse abdominis muscle is visible, and the surgeon sweeps the peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath to facilitate the TAR procedure. The linea semilunaris is identified, and the surgeon carefully sweeps the fat, occasionally using cautery to manage small vessels. The dissection continues near the linea semilunaris, avoiding the groin area to prevent lateral deviation.

The surgeon carefully approaches the seam of the linea alba where the anterior and posterior rectus sheaths converge, ensuring precise identification of anatomical landmarks. The neurovascular bundles serve as a visual guide near the linea semilunaris. Hemostasis is achieved to control any bleeding during the TAR procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the posterior rectus sheath, entering the plane between the preperitoneal fat and the posterior rectus sheath. The preperitoneal fat is swept off, revealing the peritoneum, allowing for quick division of the posterior rectus sheath. The seam of the linea semilunaris is visible, approximately one centimeter away, ensuring a safe distance during the procedure. The posterior rectus sheath is pulled and divided, with attention to the potential medialization of the linea semilunaris. The posterior rectus sheath consists of the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis aponeurosis.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the posterior rectus sheath to avoid perforating the peritoneum during the division. The dissection progresses laterally, transitioning into a plane suitable for inguinal hernia repairs, keeping the fat against the transverse abdominis and staying close to the peritoneum. The approach aims to hug the peritoneum as the dissection moves into the lower abdomen and pelvis, aligning closely with the testicular vessels and vas deferens.

The surgeon transitions into the space between the transversalis fascia and the transversus abdominis muscle. The transversalis fascia is seen lifting off the transversus abdominis, indicating the shift from the preperitoneal plane to the pre-transversalis fascia plane. The dissection continues along the transversus abdominis muscle up to the costal margin, maintaining proximity to the diaphragm muscle under the ribs.

The surgeon carefully dissects in the pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen, ensuring to move bowel out of the way for safety. The posterior rectus sheath is visible sitting on the bowel. The surgeon performs a top-down TAR, cutting the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the muscular transversus abdominis, while being cautious of the diaphragm's contribution. The dissection proceeds behind the xiphoid fat, avoiding bleeding vessels on its edges, and ensures the diaphragm is not below the dissection plane. The muscle fibers of the diaphragm are visible in the correct plane.

The surgeon scores the path along the thick transverse abdominus muscle, using scissors to lift the muscle and applying cautery to divide it. The top-down TAR is connected to the bottom-up TAR, with spot coagulation to manage bleeding. On the right side, the surgeon works under the diaphragm, while on the left, the space is still being developed, aiming to stay close to the diaphragm and transverse abdominis muscles. The bridging fascia is divided to combine the two spaces, maintaining proximity to the muscle despite tempting tissue planes.

The surgeon completes the division of the transversus abdominis muscle and proceeds with lateral retromuscular dissection. The colon is carefully pulled away to facilitate safer dissection. The surgeon cauterizes directly on the edge of the muscle, avoiding the transversalis fascia, to maintain hemostasis and expedite the procedure.

The surgeon cauterizes along the edge of the muscle, dissecting until the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath rest on the bowel. Laterally, the surgeon navigates into a different space, aiming to stay in the plane closer to the peritoneum. As the dissection progresses, the surgeon assesses the advancement in the posterior layer, noting tension and continuing to divide the tissue. Good retraction with the left hand reveals cotton candy-like tissue for further division, maintaining proximity to the peritoneum.

The surgeon provides an overview of the surgical field after completing the TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release). The posterior rectus sheath is shown reaching the midline, indicating completion. In the lower part, further dissection is performed, resembling a TAP (Transversus Abdominis Plane) dissection. The surgeon uses left-hand retraction to elevate tissue, creating a 'cotton candy' appearance that guides the dissection plane. The obturator space is visible, with a distinct valley indicating the dissection path.

The surgeon places three new ports and redocks to start the TAR procedure on the left side. The left retroactive space has been previously dissected and opened. The surgeon cuts the attenuated posterior rectus sheath in the left pelvis area and uses their hand to grab the peritoneum, aiming to find the plane where the preperitoneal fat separates easily from the transversus abdominis. The linea semilunaris is shown.

The surgeon demonstrates the linea semilunaris during the inguinal dissection, hugging the peritoneum and providing a view of the pubis. The left hand elevates the tissue, revealing the tissue plane where the round ligament may have been partially divided. Using scissors as a hot blade, the surgeon scores and opens the tissue planes, creating a cavity where the fat separates from the transverse abdominis muscle.

The surgeon sweeps the preperitoneal fat and peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring not to injure the linea semilunaris. The posterior rectus sheath is then divided, with careful attention to cauterizing small vessels underneath to prevent bleeding in the lateral space.

The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris and uses cautery to divide small vessels. A new port is placed medial to the linea semilunaris for further dissection. The preperitoneal dissection and posterior rectus sheath division are performed, with adjustments made to lighting for better visibility. The peritoneum appears stuck, making separation from the transversalis fascia challenging, prompting entry into the pre-transversalis fascia plane.

The surgeon carefully pushes the transversalis fascia off, staying medial to a visible neurovascular bundle. The posterior rectus sheath is divided before fully detaching the peritoneum. Blood is present, complicating the identification of the correct plane, but the surgeon attempts to join the right and left sides. Effective left-hand retraction aids in tissue exposure, allowing cautery to be used efficiently.

The surgeon uses left hand retraction to expose the tissue, allowing it to pop when cautery is applied. The dissection continues in a bottoms-up approach, moving into the muscular portion of the transverse abdominis. The surgeon then switches to a top-down approach, dividing the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and scoring the path to maintain the correct trajectory.

The surgeon uses cautery aggressively on the thick transverse abdominis muscle, inserting scissors behind it to facilitate the TAR (transversus abdominis release). The procedure continues from right to left, with the surgeon lifting the muscle and using cautery. After scoring through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, the surgeon proceeds in a bottoms-up fashion on the patient's left side, ensuring not to enter the space between the diaphragm and transversalis fascia. The surgeon explores to confirm being in the correct areolar plane against the diaphragm muscle, joining the spaces between the transverse abdominis and diaphragm.

The surgeon cauterizes along the edge of the transverse abdominis muscle, completing its division. The retromuscular dissection proceeds with visible areolar tissue and reduced transverse salus fascia between the muscle layers. The procedure is complete when the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath rest on the bowel, and the edges of the posterior rectus sheath meet.
